item.title: Example Talk
item.description:
Click on the Slides button above to view the built-in slides feature.

Slides can be added in a few ways:

  • Create slides using Wowchemy’s Slides feature and link using slides parameter in the front matter of the talk file
  • Upload an existing slide deck to static/ and link using url_slides parameter in the front matter of the talk file
  • Embed your slides (e.g. Google Slides) or presentation video on this page using shortcodes.

Further event details, including page elements such as image galleries, can be added to the body of this page.

item.title: e-Yantra Robotics Competition
item.description:
e-Yantra Robotics Competition eYRC is an international competition organized by IIT Bombay. I led a student team of four and participated in the 2020-21 edition of the competition. Our theme focussed on developing software for a 3‑D simulated quadcopter capable of package delivery in a cityscape Gazebo world. It was titled “Vitarana Drone”, literally translating to “delivery drone”. The competition spanned six months from October 2020 to March 2021 and consisted of continuous task-based evaluations with increasing difficulty and complexity. We had to use Python and ROS to develop all the software.

My team comprised three other members- Atharva Chandak, Ayush Agrawal, and Naitik Khandelwal. All of us were from BITS Pilani.

The organizers provided us with the Gazebo model of the drone and the world. The drone package had the relevant topics for controlling the PWM of the four motors. Apart from that, we had to develop all the code needed to move the drone autonomously. The drone carried various sensors like IMU, GPS, a Camera for marker detection (localization near the packages to be picked up or delivered), and four range sensors at perpendicular directions.

We employed a dual and discrete PID-based controller, each for the drone’s attitude and position. The attitude controller was cascaded in the position controller. All the proportional, integral, and derivative constants were obtained using Plotjuggler and some trial and error. We tuned the attitude controller first, and after freezing its values, tuned the position controller. IMU values (roll-pitch-yaw) were used as feedback for the attitude controller, and GPS was used as feedback for the position controller.

After making the controller good enough for our use, we developed a path planner. We were only provided with the initial location of the drone. We were given a setpoint of a marker, which was near the final goal (landing station). Once we reached the setpoint, we had to search for the landing station using marker detection and land inside the regulated area accordingly. Since we did not have access to any map of the Gazebo world, we employed a real-time planning algorithm and used a variant of the Bug 0 algorithm. We developed the code from scratch and added functionality such that the drone moves along the m-line (joining the initial location and the final setpoint) in a discrete manner. The term discrete is used as we divided the m-line into small sections of 5 meters, and the drone moved along these smaller sections in a start-stop manner while hovering momentarily. Thus the m-line was divided into a path of many setpoints. As soon as an obstacle was detected using any of the range sensors, a new setpoint was calculated in the direction perpendicular to that of the range sensor which detected the obstacle. After moving towards the new setpoint, the same procedure was repeated, and the m-line was modified such that the new starting position was the latest calculated set point. Note that the set point might have been inside some obstacle boundary. Still, the drone never collided as the range sensor in that direction would have detected the obstacle, and a new setpoint would have been calculated in the perpendicular direction.

Once the planner was complete, we figured out a marker detection algorithm. While the “detection” of the marker was easily done using OpenCV, the location of the marker was unknown. So what we did was, once the drone reached the given setpoint near the marker, it went up to increase the area scanned by the camera. Once the marker was detected, it came down, went near it, and landed successfully inside a rectangular boundary denoting the regulated area. After the controller and the planner was developed and integrated successfully, we had to incorporate the package lifting and delivering mechanism using an attached gripper. The gripper was controlled using an automated ROS Service, which needed to be called by us through our code. Since the package itself had some weight, the controller initially failed to carry it successfully without toppling. We sorted out this issue by tuning the controllers again, with the difference being that the drone was carrying the package in this case. Since the gripper was controlled using a service, we had a way to know if the drone was carrying a package currently. Thus, whenever it wasn’t carrying a package, we used the PID parameter values we had figured out initially and switched to the new values whenever the drone detected a parcel. Thus, our drone could now successfully deliver packages.

The final task of the competition involved optimizing the number of drones delivered in a given amount of time. A reward was calculated using the distance travelled by the drone and the number of packages delivered successfully, and the organizers used the effective reward to rank the teams. We were given the locations of the setpoints near all the parcels (which had a marker so that it could be detected using the marker-detection algorithm I mentioned above) and the delivery locations of the parcels. We needed to write a scheduling algorithm in order to find the order in which we could deliver packages and increase the reward.

Finally, we were ranked in the top 20 of the 450+ participating teams. Although we did not win the competition, it was a valuable experience and helped us develop our problem solving, critical thinking, and coding skills.

item.title: GenNav
item.description:
GenNav, short for ‘General Navigation’, was undertaken by the Electronics and Robotics Club at BITS Goa. As we did the project during the COVID-19 lockdown, I remotely contributed to the open-source project.

GenNav is a python library for robotics navigation algorithms and utilities commonly required in navigation stacks. The library aims to be completely modular and have a unified API so that it is usable in a broad range of applications and easily extensible to new robotics domains. We further extended the project to construct a ROS Wrapper on top of Gennav, called ‘gennav_ros’. ‘gennav_ros’ can be used if someone wishes to use ‘gennav’ in a ROS based stack. Since the package is open-sourced using PyPi here, GenNav can also be installed using pip install gennav, although installation from source is recommended. Rudimentary documentation for the project can be found here.

As part of the team, I implemented path planning algorithms like Rapidly‐exploring Random Tree (RRT), Rapidly‐exploring Random Graph (RRG), and some utility classes and methods for implementing Graphs, etc. While implementing the algorithms from scratch was challenging (albeit fun), the more important thing to take care of was maintaining the API across all the path planners and making all generalised pieces of code ‘modular’. I also made Jupyter and Google Colab notebook tutorials for showing how to use the available algorithms. The notebooks can be found here.

item.title: Human Authentication System using Forehead Wrinkles
item.description:
The project was based on the study of various feature detectors and image matching algorithms applied on the forehead dataset. As a part of Deep Learning course, I built a computer vision based forehead based human authentication on mobile and hand held devices. The project covers the use of SIFT, SURF, ORB, ArcFace, and AKAZE algorithms for matching images. Image Preprocessing (CLAHE) was performed before applying the matching algorithms. I achieved an average accuracy of 59.99%, and best accuracy of 73.997% using the ArcFace algorithm. A genuine-imposter histogram was plotted for each algorithm. The generated genuine and histogram datasets were used to plot the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve of FRR (X-Axis) vs FRR (Y-Axis). Correct Recognition Rate (CRR) and Equal Error Rate (EER) were calculated from the resulting ROC curve.

P.S.: This project was done as a part of the Deep Learning course offered at BITS Pilani. The detailed report of the project can be found here.

item.title: Path Planner and Autonomous Controller for a Robot
item.description:
This is a project based on path planning and control theory of robots. What this means is that I need to plan a path from the given start to goal points through a given environment using one of the path planning algorithms. I then need to create a code for a controller for the robot. This will enable the robot to traverse the path in a ‘point-by-point’ manner to reach the goal. The environment in which my project runs consists of a series of static obstacles and the robot. Trotbot (a robot model) has been used to test my code. Trotbot is based on the Omnibase package. Both are developed by the Electronics and Robotics Club, BITS Goa.

I implemented a PID controller and a Randomly Exploring Random Tree (RRT) path planner for a Trotbot robot. The Trotbot autonomously planned its way through a series of static obstacles and traversed the resultant path. The code was written using Python, and ROS was used as the framework. Each entity with different functionality (e.g.: controller, path planner) was written as a ROS Node. The Publisher-Subscriber, or Pub-Sub, model in ROS was used to set up the communication between the nodes.

Structure of the project:

  1. Obstacle detector node which publishes a fixed list of obstacles. Here I published the list of centre points of the cylindrical obstacles.
  2. Planner node which subscribes to obstacles and publishes a path.
  3. Controller node which subscribes to the path and the current pose of the robot. It calculates the required velocity of the robot, and publishes it on the corresponding topic. This controller will traverse the path one point at a time using PID Controller.

All the code was tested on the robot using simulations on Gazebo. More information about the project can be found here.

P.S.: This project was done as a part of the QSTP Course Project. QSTP (Quark Summer Term Projects) is an annual event where the technical clubs from BITS Goa offer introductory courses in their respective domains. My introduction to robotics was this very project, offered in May 2020 during the COVID-19 lockdown. The course page can be found here. Since it was my first door to the vast field of robotics, this project holds a special position in my heart. I’ll always be grateful to the course mentors, whose efforts are the reason I have come this far in robotics.

item.title: Self Balancing two wheeled robot
item.description:
A strictly two-wheeled robot (without a free castor wheel for support) provides a helpful platform for testing complex control algorithms. It helps ensure that a multi-variable system will be physically stable. In this project, we study the use of the modern Linear Quadratic Regulator controller for balancing the robot and extend the application for the robot to traverse any path. A differential drive mechanism is used while developing the robot, which helps decouple the translation and rotation stages in path traversal. An A* algorithm has been used for path planning in a static obstacle occupied environment. It is shown that an LQR controlled robot can successfully help the robot stabilize on the spot without falling and enable linear motion in the forward direction.

P.S.: This project was done as a part of the Autonomous Mobile Robotics course offered at BITS Pilani. The project report can be found here.

item.title: Big Data for Big Problems
item.description: Head over to this link to read my blog.

item.title: Towards a Digital Inida- The UPI Way!
item.description:
Let us rewind five years of our life. We live in the era of Internet Banking- the supposedly ‘better’ alternative to offline and physical means of banking. But was it? Net banking requires our account number and type, bank name, and IFSC code. Most banks add a new payee after 12 hours, which means your friend will get the money long after he needs it. Not to mention the efforts you put in to type all this on your phone.

Enter UPI or United Payments Interface:

The very intention of introducing UPI was to get rid of all this hassle. In layman’s terms, UPI is nothing but an email-id for your money. Something which your bank uses to do money transfers and payments. UPI uses Immediate Payment Service (IMPS) to do this. IMPS is faster than NEFT, as its acts immediately, and also, it’s 24x7x365. Thus, UPI makes it easier to make online payments than a digital wallet or credit or debit card.

Let’s look at the architecture of UPI.

  1. You link your UPI wallet with a bank, after which you’ll get a virtual address for the same.
  2. You use this virtual address while making a payment via your bank.
  3. UPI would use the information it has to map the virtual address of the sending and receiving banks to the existing bank accounts and then initiate a credit and debit transaction.

Impact on the Country:

Due to its perks, UPI is in progress to overtake the other online wallets. Even if the latter outperforms the former on a cumulative basis, the UPI app is growing faster per month. In February this year, transactions exceeding Rs.1.01 lakh crore (that’s over $14 billion) were recorded via UPI, far above mobile wallets Rs.14,279 crore.

UPI deserves this credit as it has three significant advantages over online wallets:

  1. Simpler to use
  2. No KYC (know your customer) KYC is not required for UPI as it is just like any other internet banking transaction.
  3. Interoperability- You can’t use Paytm to send money to your friend using Google Pay. UPI overcomes this.

This has forced online wallets to make themselves more lucrative by offering many cash backs. While this has benefited them, it has also increased the bank’s activities, as ultimately, the bank is where the real money lies. Thus, the introduction of UPI has benefitted both parties, while the people are the most significant gainers.

Above all, UPI would be good for the economy as it would give way to mass cashless payments. The use of hard cash has come down, and the economy has become more transparent. Digital payments are growing quickly within the country; however, they’re still a fraction of the payments. Most of the transactions involving small amounts of money are still in cash. The ease and convenience UPI offers has helped individuals prefer to pay their maids, milkman, newspaper vendor, etc., digitally than in cash. Today, many find bank visits cumbersome. With UPI, we feel the same about visiting an ATM. There are over 40 million merchants in India, and out of those, only about a million use card readers. With UPI, they won’t even need those archaic machines. Not to forget the costs per transaction that come with the likes of VISA, MasterCard, etc. UPI costs only 50 paise per transaction. All in all, the impact UPI has had and will continue to have on our country is too large to neglect.

Looking Ahead: What UPI Can be:

There is one feature in UPI, which is ‘mandates’. Mandates or standing instructions mean that UPI can become the go-to payment option for all recurring payments like SIPs, Mutual Fund payments, monthly subscriptions etc. Also, the withdrawal of the biometric Aadhaar-based payment feature will make it unusable for those who do not own a smartphone, i.e. about 30% of India’s population is deprived of the choice to use UPI or any other digital payment solution. So, bringing these back will only boost UPI.

A Green Approach to do that:

Every year we produce more than 2 million tonnes of e-waste, and the majority comes from used mobile phones. We can build our operating system (O.S.) having the features of calling, texting, camera, and UPI based app. We will load this on our used phone. Selling these to the poverty-ridden sector at a low price will only help widen the reach of UPI while also reducing e-waste. I’ll conclude by saying that for UPI to become the leader in digital transactions, it must drive large-scale adoption of digital payments. There needs to be an extensive merchant-acceptance network, both online and offline because that is where the real push for ‘Digital India’ will come from.

item.title: Slides
item.description:
Create slides in Markdown with Wowchemy

Wowchemy | Documentation

Features

  • Efficiently write slides in Markdown
  • 3-in-1: Create, Present, and Publish your slides
  • Supports speaker notes
  • Mobile friendly slides

Controls

  • Next: Right Arrow or Space
  • Previous: Left Arrow
  • Start: Home
  • Finish: End
  • Overview: Esc
  • Speaker notes: S
  • Fullscreen: F
  • Zoom: Alt + Click
  • PDF Export: E

Code Highlighting

Inline code: variable

Code block:

porridge = "blueberry"
if porridge == "blueberry":
    print("Eating...")

Math

In-line math: $x + y = z$

Block math:

$$ f\left( x \right) = ;\frac{{2\left( {x + 4} \right)\left( {x - 4} \right)}}{{\left( {x + 4} \right)\left( {x + 1} \right)}} $$

Fragments

Make content appear incrementally

{{% fragment %}} One {{% /fragment %}}
{{% fragment %}} **Two** {{% /fragment %}}
{{% fragment %}} Three {{% /fragment %}}

Press Space to play!

One Two Three

A fragment can accept two optional parameters:

  • class: use a custom style (requires definition in custom CSS)
  • weight: sets the order in which a fragment appears

Speaker Notes

Add speaker notes to your presentation

{{% speaker_note %}}
- Only the speaker can read these notes
- Press `S` key to view
{{% /speaker_note %}}

Press the S key to view the speaker notes!

  • Only the speaker can read these notes
  • Press S key to view

Themes

  • black: Black background, white text, blue links (default)
  • white: White background, black text, blue links
  • league: Gray background, white text, blue links
  • beige: Beige background, dark text, brown links
  • sky: Blue background, thin dark text, blue links
  • night: Black background, thick white text, orange links
  • serif: Cappuccino background, gray text, brown links
  • simple: White background, black text, blue links
  • solarized: Cream-colored background, dark green text, blue links

Custom Slide

Customize the slide style and background

{{< slide background-image="/media/boards.jpg" >}}
{{< slide background-color="#0000FF" >}}
{{< slide class="my-style" >}}

Custom CSS Example

Let’s make headers navy colored.

Create assets/css/reveal_custom.css with:

.reveal section h1,
.reveal section h2,
.reveal section h3 {
  color: navy;
}

Questions?

Ask

Documentation

item.title: An example conference paper
item.description:
Click the Cite button above to demo the feature to enable visitors to import publication metadata into their reference management software.
Create your slides in Markdown - click the Slides button to check out the example.

Supplementary notes can be added here, including code, math, and images.
